title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several key items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a $126,900 contract to King Gravel for recycled asphalt crushing, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road utilities and construction services for the Alderson/Jelnik roundabout and Bladel Avenue projects.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll Call
Public Comment
Approval of April 13, 2026 Meeting Minutes
Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permit
CIP Update
Street Operation Update
Utility Operating Update
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC Annual Report
Resolution 2026-014 Transportation Funding
Recycled Asphalt Base Course Production
Sanitary Sewer Discharge Violation Discussion
Water and Sewer Connection Agreement for Radkey Avenue
Military Road Construction Agreement
Construction Service Proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. This 2029 project may be moved up to 2028. The village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue road repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating, Schofield Avenue pavement repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street restoration by Melvin. A special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue was set for Thursday, May 28th at 6:00 PM.

Staff reported road limits were removed on April 29th with Marathon County. Work included mailbox repairs, sign repairs, spring curb sweeping, and hot mix patching. Dewatering pumps operated for about a week near Weston Avenue tributary to prevent flooding at Crane Meadows. Staff discussed potential permanent pump solutions for recurring flooding issues. Culverts failed on Jones Street requiring additional repairs. Equipment maintenance and seasonal changeovers were completed on multiple vehicles.

Josh reported on a service repair at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and ongoing meter card maintenance. Significant clear water infiltration was identified - in April, 37 million gallons entered the system but 53-54 million gallons were treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified sump pump discharges into sanitary sewers as a major contributor. An INI study by In-Pipe Technologies will help identify problem areas. Water main flushing notices were being mailed with flushing to begin the following week.

discussions.item: Review 2025 Water Utility PSC Annual Report
discussions.body: Michael presented the annual PSC report showing net operating income of $482,911, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. Utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service including Wells 7 and 8 and water main from Weston Avenue project. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. PFAS settlement funds of $1.44 million were received. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution seeks long-term sustainable funding for state transportation. While the village has been successful obtaining grants for one-time projects, recurring operational funding remains inadequate, requiring subsidies from general tax dollars. Wisconsin's automobile ownership costs compare favorably to neighboring states. The committee recommended the village board adopt the resolution; motion passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed quotes for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton versus $4.25/ton from competitor. For estimated 30,000 tons, total cost is $126,900. Michael noted this practice saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, equating to about $130,000 savings on three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement of sewer discharge violations, particularly illegal sump pump connections. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee members discussed distinguishing between unknowing versus willful violations. Trustee Zagami suggested waiting for the INI study results, then sending notices giving 30-60 days to correct. The committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100/day for first offenses. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement for Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a builder wants to construct a duplex on lot 1 of Radkey Avenue where water/sewer mains currently dead-end. Rather than require immediate main extension, staff proposed allowing a temporary long lateral connection until Radkey Avenue reconstruction when the owner would pay assessments. Committee added language requiring reconstruction 'no later than 2029.' Motion to recommend board authorization passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road reconstruction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Rothschild holds the contract with Steam Construction. Weston's share for water main relocation (conflicting with storm sewer improvements) and sanitary sewer is estimated at $94,860. Motion to recommend board approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730. Staff recommended MSA for the roundabout (they assisted with design) and Clark Dietz for Bladel (full-time experienced staff versus MSA's summer intern). Committee approved both recommendations unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900 total)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day
Authorize water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with assessment due upon reconstruction no later than 2029
Recommend board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860 utility costs
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelnik construction services ($37,315) and Clark Dietz for Bladel construction services ($73,730)
Special assessment meeting scheduled for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM for Bladel Avenue project
Norcon to begin concrete repairs on Schofield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday
Staff to investigate street lighting options for Schofield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission also heard that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield has been canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Election of officers
Public comments
Approval of minutes from April 21st meeting
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members
Adjournment

discussions.body: The commission elected new officers for the reorganized body. Trustee Erling was nominated for chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for vice chair by a commission member, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and approved unanimously as secretary.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill from 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that the competing Marshfield hot air balloon event has been canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. A commissioner asked about hotel room usage, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission requested better tracking of hotel rooms from the Holiday Inn Express to help justify future funding decisions. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request for detailed room usage in their post-event report. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting the winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees. She mentioned this was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s and the candidates were impressed with the community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide hotel room usage data from Holiday Inn Express in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative time)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and established a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
April 2026 building permits
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Commissioner remarks
Future agenda items
Adjournment

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility Site Plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline constraint, which allowed it to be placed on higher ground and reduced fill costs. Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth, up from current staffing of 40 officers. Discussion included the alternate storage shed placement and potential for future building expansion to the north. Motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt Storage Shed Inspection Program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff proposed a program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter inspection focusing on material handling and containment. Jennifer noted they identified one existing facility near Sternberg that never went through the wellhead protection conditional use process and is closest to the village well. Commissioners agreed to reach out to that facility about compliance. Motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.

discussions.item: April 2026 Staff Approved Items and Building Permits
discussions.body: Both the April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps/site plans and the April 2026 building permits were approved unanimously without discussion.

Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Discussion followed about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with staff noting Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense on landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing relaxed standards applied to the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested that the salt storage shed inspection program include annual reporting back to the commission on site visits and findings.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department approved with conditions 2-5, forwarded to Board of Trustees
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation in wellhead protection district
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg about wellhead protection compliance
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invite for June 1st steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station item added to future agenda for fall
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No formal policy votes were taken; the meeting focused on introducing committee members to department missions, structures, budgets, and current initiatives.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (noting Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg remote)
Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute (no items for this meeting)
Review of standing committee ordinances, missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all in favor.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction under ordinance section 2.04 sub5, covering the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and affiliated entities including North Central Health Care and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He outlined reporting relationships with statutory boards including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the upcoming strategic plan development expected by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting from environmental hazards. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen for restaurants, pools, and hotels; communicable disease response including recent tick kit distribution through free public health vending machines; the Nurse Family Partnership program now graduating its first two-year-old cohort; and a new FTE position for homeless system response. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE staff across four divisions: environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administration. Administrator Leonard noted about half the $5 million budget is tax levy, with the rest from public charges for services and intergovernmental grants.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. The department has nearly 140 FTE handling child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. Of their $22 million budget, approximately $7 million is tax levy. Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program addressing housing instability root causes, noting many participants had never had bank accounts or formal leases before. He indicated upcoming restructuring requests may come forward due to increased need in certain units.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported his office of 3.0 FTE had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up significantly from prior years. He highlighted $44 million annually in VA benefits coming to county veterans, plus $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability work with Center for Veterans Issues establishing a local office, and suicide prevention efforts noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans. Galindo confirmed the office can help any veteran regardless of county residence due to federal accreditation. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional collaboration; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have a VSO and his office maintains good relationships with neighboring counties using compatible systems like VetPro.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county ADRC serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties. About 11% of their budget comes from county levy. He described services including information and assistance, dementia care specialists, falls prevention through the 'Stepping On' program, Medicare/Medicaid benefit specialists, and the Meals on Wheels nutrition program. Ray emphasized significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs up 40-45% in four years while federal Older Americans Act funding remains flat at 2024 levels. The organization has approximately 75 staff plus 385-400 volunteers. The ADRC had not requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate meal program at the Hmong American Center.

actionItems: Minutes from April 1st meeting approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion items to upcoming meetings
Committee members invited to submit agenda items via email to Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bicycle regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public comments
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
Approval of previous meeting minutes
Acknowledgment of reports from boards, committees, and commissions
Acknowledgment of department reports
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances - mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bicycles
New business - committee appointments and remote meeting policy amendments
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the lack of real operational data to justify the numbers. Trustee Barb supported the fee increase citing population growth and fairness. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark. Both passed unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained that Safer and multiple municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and Clover created a reciprocal inspection agreement for food trucks. One fire inspection by any participating department places a sticker on the truck valid for all communities, eliminating the need for multiple inspections. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously with motion by Lope, second by Zagami.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting policy. Several trustees wanted to remove specific language about health, safety, and welfare justifications, preferring a broader policy. Trustee Beck moved to amend by striking sentences requiring specific justifications. The amendment passed with two no votes from Trustees Hermling and Lope Sorrell. The original motion as amended then passed with the same two dissenting votes.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lope Sorrell, passed unanimously. Parks, Finance/HR, and CLIPS still have openings. The board agreed to include committee applicant resumes in future packets.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park after being displaced from the Camp Phillips/Scoffield corner by development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about parking on grass along Alderson. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors appreciate having their vehicles with them. Mount Miller Park was discussed but rejected due to its remote location. The board acknowledged this is a discussion-only item with no action taken.

Public Works Director Michael explained that two engineering consultant contracts totaling approximately $108,000 are needed for project oversight because the engineering technician position remains unfilled. He noted the specific skill set required is in short supply in central Wisconsin, with even consulting firms pulling staff from southeastern Wisconsin offices.

The board approved a secondhand article dealer and secondhand jewelry dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained this involves buying and selling precious metals with large dollar amounts. The approval was amended to be contingent on satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit. Passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 5200 N 52nd Street spoke during public comment criticizing the $108,000 expenditure for outside engineering consultants, questioning why the engineering technician position remains unfilled. He also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer meeting before being officially appointed. He requested better documentation on Kennedy Park project status and called for more administrator accountability.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with ordinance 26-010 and resolution 2026-015
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Mobile food vendor reciprocal inspection ordinance 26-008 adopted
E-bicycle ordinance 26-012 adopted amending chapter 82 traffic and vehicles
Nuisance ordinance 26-011 adopted
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC secondhand dealer license approved contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Comprehensive plan steering committee first meeting scheduled for Monday June 1st at 6:00 PM
Staff to include committee applicant resumes in future board packets
Weston Avenue speed limit study to be brought back to the board
Staff to develop intergovernmental service agreement for building inspection assistance to Town of Ringle

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the executive committee, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris related to the recent tornado and flooding disaster. The board heard emotional public testimony from Ringle residents affected by the severe weather requesting the county waive disposal fees entirely.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Roll call and sign-in
Committee appointments introduction and remarks from Chair
Election of at-large executive committee members
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26: Social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
Vote on landfill tipping fee resolution
Announcements and adjournment

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks thanking Vice Chair Boots for assistance in developing committee appointments, emphasizing goals of increasing diverse perspectives, developing supervisor leadership, and honoring supervisor preferences. He noted 34% of the board is in its first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated for two at-large positions: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained her nomination of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's 'smallest children and oldest elders.' Paper ballots were distributed and counted. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on a motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but not unanimously with no discussion recorded.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but not unanimously with no discussion recorded.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to Landfill Tipping Fees
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris from the recent tornado and flooding. The standard $65/ton gate rate would be reduced to $36.94/ton for certified storm debris, reflecting waivers from the state ($13.10) and Town of Ringle ($2.33), plus a county reduction to cover only operational costs. Leonard noted Marathon County won't meet the $670,743 threshold for state disaster aid but expects Ringle will. He discussed potential partnership with the Community Foundation to help residents with unreimbursed disposal costs. After public testimony and extensive board discussion, the rules were suspended unanimously to bring the resolution forward without committee review. The resolution was then approved unanimously on a motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Tolesie advocated for Marathon County to waive fees entirely, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are all waiving their portions. She emphasized the community has been self-reliant, with businesses donating up to $40,000 in cleanup efforts, and requested relief for six months. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, noting 44 homes are affected, many insurance adjusters haven't arrived yet, and residents need more time and relief for unreimbursed costs.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to executive committee at-large positions
Landfill tipping fees reduced to $36.94/ton for certified disaster-related debris through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for residents' unreimbursed disposal costs
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop a clear policy within six months for handling similar disaster situations in the future
Board to revisit tipping fee extension at May meeting if needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees (not other zoning or septic fees) for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from the Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public comment
Approval of March 31, 2026 committee minutes
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes with Supervisor Drebeck seconding. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for, against, or as interested. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone would make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve and forward to county board. Supervisor Drew seconded. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather damage
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17 tornado affecting the towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Significant debate ensued. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future incidents. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to individuals who experience damage outside declared disaster areas. Supervisor Sefeld questioned potential levy impacts. Eric Vogel asked if homeowners insurance would cover fees. Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other zoning or septic fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment carried unanimously. The amended resolution then passed on a non-unanimous vote to forward to the county board.

Director David Hagenbusher presented an educational overview of the Solid Waste Department, covering the landfill history since 1980, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges due to PFAS regulations requiring treatment by 2032, and significant capital expenses including a $6 million 11-acre cell construction project. Marilyn Bend praised the department's work during the Ringle tornado response.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie who had an emergency. He covered the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees, the joint city-county park operation since 1924, and the 30,700 acres of county forest. He noted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced that Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service. Joe Tucker was selected as the new county forest administrator.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution to Marathon County Board of Supervisors (waiving only reconnect fees for septic systems for tornado-damaged properties)
Forward Tim Veland/James Offer/David Tract rezoning petition to county board for approval
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition to county board for approval
Town of Green Valley recommended to update comprehensive plan to reflect zoning change if applicable
Town of McMillan recommended to update comprehensive plan to reflect zoning change if applicable

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks and Recreation Committee approved installation of new kayak launch signs for the Plover River and deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until the next meeting. The committee received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising progress and discussed maintenance challenges for graffiti removal on the Oaklair River Trail.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Approval of minutes from April 27th, 2026
Public comments
Kennedy Park update
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs
Topics for future meetings
Staff remarks and announcements

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27th, 2026 meeting were approved. A motion was made and seconded with no discussion. The motion carried unanimously.

Staff presented a timeline document showing the project's history from the comprehensive plan update through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Director Sean Oster explained the village had planned infrastructure improvements worth approximately $7 million of the $14.3 million total project. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about funding responsibilities, with staff explaining the Friends of Kennedy Park nonprofit handles fundraising with no fixed deadline, though a 10-year evaluation period is referenced in their agreement. Staff noted the Friends group has approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation toward a $117,000 goal for field lights and plans a public check presentation at their June tournament.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff provided photos and a PDF showing locations of fallen trees in the river and the installation of wood mats across gas pipelines for equipment access. Dan explained the mats were placed to protect two TC Energy gas pipelines during logging operations. The committee reviewed photos of the mat installation process using heavy equipment.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The item was deferred to next month's meeting because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy attended specifically to oppose dogs at the pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' His objection was noted and he was encouraged to return next month.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches on the trail, noting their best tool (a sandblaster pressure washer) cannot access the trail due to bridge weight limits and steep ditches. Committee members discussed potential solutions including anti-vandalism coatings and volunteer workdays. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan from Marathon County presented version 7 of informational signs for Plover River kayak launches. Three signs will be installed in Weston at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks locations. The signs include a river map, QR code for river gauge, and location-specific history. Dan noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. The committee approved the signs unanimously for installation and to forward to the village board.

Staff requested committee interest in food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds, explaining a community member offered to provide signs. The committee agreed to bring the item back for future discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler, and the final bike and pedestrian plan open house is June 11th from 5-7 PM.

publicComment: Two residents provided public comment. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) attended in person to oppose dogs at the aquatic center, stating dogs should not be allowed in a public pool and belong in rivers and lakes. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston) spoke online, criticizing the 4 PM meeting time as discouraging public participation, and questioning Kennedy Park fundraising finances, calling the $8,700 expense figure 'too coincidental' and demanding transparency on where grant money and Glow event proceeds went.
actionItems: Kayak launch signs approved for installation at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks - to be forwarded to village board
Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate graffiti removal and cleaning options for Oaklair River Trail
Food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds to be brought back for future discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22nd, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April budget and financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, including elections, licensing, tax collection, and the village newsletter. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
April budget status report
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Legal details report for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's office budget and operations
Old business, new business, and staff remarks
Adjournment

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. No discussion. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports
discussions.body: Jessica provided an update on TIF district closure, anticipating October-November timeline. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds due October 1st. A letter will be sent to US Bank Associate Trust and forwarded to DTC for bondholder payments. Committee member confirmed the final numbers still look good and the village will be able to pay off the debt.
The April budget status report, TIFF detail reports, and legal details report were acknowledged together. Motion to acknowledge made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented on the clerk's office operations, staffing, and budget. The office has moved from four full-time staff to two full-time and one part-time employee. Key functions include election administration (with $11,000 spent on four new Badger Books), licensing (51 liquor licenses, numerous bartender licenses), and tax collection (84% of the $22 million collected goes through the clerk's office by walk-ins). Pet licensing was brought back in-house in 2025 after 12 years with a third party, saving approximately $5,495 annually. The village newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing to reach 5,089 postal patrons.

Pam noted that election chief inspectors are paid on the lower end compared to other Marathon County municipalities at $13/hour versus the average. She indicated she will likely propose increasing the rate to $15/hour when the next two-year election term begins, citing the significant responsibilities of the position.

Discussion addressed why pet licensing fees doubled from $10 to $20 for neutered dogs. The increase was requested by Marathon County, which pays approximately $100,000 annually to the Humane Society but was only collecting about $65,000 from municipalities. The county suggested municipalities raise fees to cover the shortfall.

Committee discussed costs associated with the Mountain Bay Metro non-dog stray contract, noting that eight mice were picked up last year at approximately $175 per mouse. A committee member suggested requiring law enforcement to bring in strays rather than allowing residents to do so, as other municipalities with similar contracts require.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well, with full vendor capacity including food trucks, coffee vendors, and craft vendors. Some concerns were raised about parking on Alderson Street and Newport, with clarification that only the west side of Alderson allows parking while Newport has never had parking restrictions.
Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked fellow committee members for the experience.

actionItems: CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIF bonds due October 1st
Pam to consider increasing chief inspector pay to $15/hour for next election term
Staff to continue monitoring farmers market parking situation
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee spots
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to chapter 50 nuisances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on e-motos, and authorized demolition proceedings for a blighted property. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring decisions on both items pending further staff research.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Written communications and department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Proposed amendments to chapter 50 nuisances
E-bike ordinance in chapter 82 traffic and vehicles
Overnight parking regulations October 31st to May 1st
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street near Mount Olive Church
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements
Address corrections for E911 compliance
Raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue

discussions.body: Roman/Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement role after the property sale was completed and new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported 2,394 police calls in March (77 per day) with 410 traffic stops and 281 citations issued. The police department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th starting in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer for Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. The committee acknowledged reports 5, 6, and 7 by motion from Chwanka, seconded by Olsen, passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments to chapter 50, including previously discussed changes to tree limb heights over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet) with language requiring compliance with jurisdictions that have authority. Olsen made a motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee discussed the proposed e-bike ordinance for chapter 82. Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting e-motos from roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee member Mike Compeer raised concerns about electric scooters used by middle schoolers. After extensive discussion about definitions and enforcement discretion, Olsen moved to recommend approval with the addition that e-motos are prohibited from operating on roadways, sidewalks, trails, or any public property in the village. Chwanka seconded. Motion passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4 AM to 4 PM) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities which typically have 5-hour windows. Michael (DPW) indicated he could work with eliminating overnight parking and instead signing 6-8 specific problem areas. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight restrictions, preferring uniformity with other municipalities. The committee deferred action, directing Michael to identify specific exception areas to bring back in July or August before next winter season.

Mount Olive Church requested an additional crosswalk between the church and DC Everest school. Staff estimated cost at approximately $2,000 for a basic crosswalk or $25,000-$30,000 for a lighted system. Committee members expressed reluctance to set precedent of paying for crosswalks on request. Olsen moved that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the installation cost, the committee recommends board approval. Chwanka seconded. Motion passed unanimously. Michael will contact Mount Olive about cost-sharing.

Chief Hunt explained a regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and SAFER where any participating department can inspect a food truck and the sticker is recognized by all. Fire Marshal Marty already conducted one inspection that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Wausau declined to participate, preferring their own licensing. Chwanka moved to approve ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors requiring correction: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing second address), 3917 Ross Avenue (out of sequence with existing apartment building at 3909), and 2211 Radtke Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Chief Hunt added another addressing issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski with eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on a structure abandoned since 2010-2011. A planned fire burn training exercise was cancelled because the structure was too deteriorated for safe training. The current owners, who are current on taxes and run a small farm operation, previously housed chickens in the structure. Owners have agreed to the raze order with costs placed on tax roll. Olsen moved, Campkey seconded. Motion passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt addressed the committee with concerns about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, predicting the grassy areas will be destroyed by vehicle parking. He questioned whether current code prohibits parking on grass and noted ADA accessible parking spaces are placed on grass rather than the adjacent asphalt, creating mobility issues. He also questioned the new fire inspection requirement for mobile food vendors, asked about food truck permitting on public property, and criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, stating some have been on the list for years with no visible progress.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended to board of trustees for approval
E-bike ordinance with e-moto prohibition on public property recommended to board of trustees
Michael to identify 6-8 specific areas requiring special parking signage for winter parking regulations, bring back in July/August
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church about covering crosswalk installation costs
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved and recommended to board
Address corrections for E911 compliance recommended to board of trustees
Staff authorized to begin raze order proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster in yard
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

Approve the Minutes
Audit of the Bills
Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Public and Student Comment

Recommendation for Preliminary 2026-27 Budget (Action Requested)
2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update (Action Requested)
Activities & Athletics Code (Action Requested)
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)
Riverview RFP Approval (Action Requested)
Adjourn

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions

Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Preliminary Discussion Regarding Potential Litigation 19.85 (g)
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Roll Call
Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Reading of the Mission Statement
Excellence in Action: Maine Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Approve Consent Agenda (Action Requested)
Old/Recurring Business
New Business
Open Forum
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Arbor Day Proclamation
Week of the Young Child Proclamation
National Preservation Month Proclamation
Workers Memorial Day Proclamation
March 24, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
April 14, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
